findings.finding: Cross-reference mismatch: corpus §5.7 states 'Branch — covered in topic 13.12' and 'Merge — covered in topic 13.12', but both branch and merge are fully taught in 13.11 (position 2). The reading.md echoes the same error ('Branch and merge — covered in topic 13.12'). This is a forward-reference label error — the content is not missing, the pointer is wrong.
findings.recommendation: Update §5.7 of topic_corpus.md and the corresponding paragraph in reading.md to read 'Branch and merge — covered in topic 13.11.'

findings.finding: API key, python-dotenv, os.getenv(), and environment variable appear as core concepts in both 13.13 (§5.4) and 13.14 (§5.1, §5.4, §5.6). Noun-set Jaccard ≈ 0.22 — below the 0.6 hard-fail threshold. The framing is deliberately differentiated (13.13: organisational tool; 13.14: security consequences + depth). No action required; noted for author awareness.

Union of LOs across 13.10–13.15 fully covers all stated module outcome facets: motivation/why VCS (13.10), core Git mechanics — repo/commit/branch/merge (13.11), GitHub remote workflow — clone/add/commit/push (13.12), professional project organisation — folder layout/README/.gitignore/requirements.txt (13.13), secret management and security consequences (13.14), atomic commit discipline and commit message conventions (13.15). No uncovered facets detected.

13.13 §5.4 introduces .gitignore, API key, the .env file pattern, and os.getenv() in the context of project organisation. 13.14 re-introduces and deepens all four from a security-consequences angle. Jaccard ≈ 0.22, well below the 0.6 threshold. The topical overlap is intentional and pedagogically sound (breadth-first introduction → depth-focused follow-up). No action required.
13.15 deepens 'commit' and 'commit message' from 13.11. Jaccard ≈ 0.11 — well below threshold. Appropriate progressive deepening, not duplication.

lab_activity_alignment.lab_seed: Create a GitHub repository for your Capstone. Commit with a proper README and .gitignore. Make at least 3 meaningful commits.

supported_by: 13.12 — §6 Implementation: full step-by-step create → clone → add → commit → push; Hands-On Exercise builds the exact sequence
13.13 — §5.3 five-section README template; §6 Implementation Step 7 writes the README; Hands-On Exercise produces the README as part of A4 submission
13.13 — §5.4 .gitignore introduced with ordering rule and practical template
13.14 — security consequences + .env.example pattern deepens the rule
13.15 — §9 Hands-On Exercise explicitly produces 3 atomic commits with meaningful messages (README, .gitignore, requirements.txt); atomic commit rule taught in §5.1

findings.finding: pip, virtual environment, and Python import syntax (from dotenv import load_dotenv) first appear in this module in 13.13 without a cross-module citation. These are M5-module-external prerequisites expected from earlier weeks. Within this module they are used without forward-teaching, which is correct for week 13 of M5. The corpus.critic for 13.14 already logged a soft note ('verify pip introduced in prior M5 topic'). No action needed within this module; the M5 course-drift critic should confirm the prior introduction.

git rm --cached .env appears in §5.5 as a recovery step if .gitignore was added too late. The command is used without a step-by-step explanation of what 'un-staging' means at the Git internals level. Given the atomic profile and the remediation context, the brief inline treatment ('You must un-stage it with git rm --cached .env before committing. This tells Git to stop tracking it without deleting it from your disk.') is adequate for beginners. Soft flag only — no re-teach required, but authors may wish to confirm the explanation is sufficient for the target audience.

summary.notable_finding: One soft terminology finding in 13.10: the forward-reference in §5.7 (and mirrored in reading.md) misidentifies topic 13.12 as the location where branch and merge are taught; the correct topic is 13.11. This is a label error only — the actual content is correct in 13.11. All other checks pass cleanly. Module learning arc is complete, prerequisites are respected in all six topics, no core-concept pairs exceed the 0.6 Jaccard duplication threshold, and the lab activity is fully supported.
